text: Family Planning Methods Overview: CHW Reference Card

Family planning lets a woman and her partner choose when to have a baby and how many children to have. It is a basic right. Every woman and man has the right to decide freely. Your job as a CHW is to give correct information, answer questions, and refer to the health centre for any method that needs a health worker to give or insert.
Your Role as a CHW

You can: share general information about all methods, give out condoms, help a woman understand which methods need a health worker visit, support women to go to the health centre, and answer common questions. You should NOT: choose a method for a woman, pressure anyone to use (or not use) a method, or give injections or insert IUDs — these need trained health workers.
Condoms

Male condoms (worn by the man) and female condoms (inserted by the woman) protect against both pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections (STIs) including HIV. They are the ONLY method that protects against STIs. They must be used every time and correctly. A CHW can give out condoms without a prescription.

How to use a male condom: Put on before any contact, not just before the finish. Squeeze the tip to remove air. Roll all the way down. After sex, hold the base and remove carefully. Use a new condom every time. Do not use oil-based lubricants (oil, vaseline, cooking oil) — they break latex.
Pills (Oral Contraceptives)

Pills are taken every day. There are two types: combined pills (contain two hormones — oestrogen and progestogen) and progestogen-only pills (sometimes called 'mini-pill'). Pills are safe for most women. They do not protect against HIV or STIs. A woman can get pills at the health centre without a doctor's appointment in most settings.

Pills work very well if taken every day at the same time. Missing pills increases the chance of pregnancy. A woman who forgets pills often may do better with an injection or implant. Refer to the health centre for first prescription.
Injections

An injection of hormones prevents pregnancy for 1–3 months (depending on the type). The most common type (DMPA) lasts 3 months. The woman must return every 3 months for the next injection — if she is more than 2 weeks late, she may be at risk of pregnancy. Injections are given at the health centre.
Good for women who do not want to take a pill every day. Periods may become irregular or stop — this is safe, not harmful. Does not protect against HIV or STIs. Can be used while breastfeeding.
IUD (Coil)

An IUD is a small device put inside the womb by a trained health worker. It prevents pregnancy for 5–10 years. It is one of the most effective methods. The woman can have it removed at any time if she wants to get pregnant. Getting it inserted may be uncomfortable for a short time but is safe.

A copper IUD (no hormones) can also be used as emergency contraception if inserted within 5 days of unprotected sex. This is the most effective form of emergency contraception. Only available at health centres. Does not protect against HIV or STIs.
Implants

An implant is a small flexible stick (about the size of a matchstick) put under the skin of the arm by a trained health worker. It prevents pregnancy for 3–5 years. Very effective. Can be removed at any time. Periods may become irregular. Can be used while breastfeeding. Does not protect against HIV or STIs. Only available at health centres.
Natural Methods (Fertility Awareness)

Natural methods involve tracking the days each month when a woman is most likely to get pregnant and avoiding sex (or using a condom) on those days. These methods require careful tracking and consistent use to work. They are less effective than hormonal methods or the IUD. They do NOT protect against HIV or STIs. If a woman is interested, refer to the health centre for proper training.
Emergency Contraception (EC)

Emergency contraception ('morning-after pill') prevents pregnancy after unprotected sex. It must be taken as soon as possible — within 5 days (120 hours) — the sooner the better. It is NOT an abortion pill — it prevents pregnancy from starting. It can be used after a condom breaks, after sexual violence, or when no method was used.

EC pills are available at health centres and sometimes at pharmacies. Refer women who need EC URGENTLY — do not delay. If the woman was raped, she also needs other care — refer to the health centre right away (see GBV Referral Pathway card).
When to Refer to a Health Centre

Refer to the health centre if: (1) She wants to start pills, injection, IUD, or implant — a health worker must screen her. (2) She has questions you cannot answer. (3) She has side effects she is worried about (heavy bleeding, pain, no period for a long time). (4) She wants to stop a method. (5) She needs emergency contraception. (6) She thinks she might be pregnant while using a method.
⚠️ Remember: It is her choice. A woman should never be forced or pressured to use any method — or to not use one. Respect her decision. Give correct information and let her choose.
